turns.user_message: Look, I work at a dhaba and everyone smokes here. It's part of the culture. I don't really want to quit, but the doctor insisted I come.
step2_rag_chunks: Avoid situations where others are smoking during the early stages of quitting. Being around smokers can increase the urge to smoke.

Avoid keeping cigarettes, lighters, or ashtrays nearby when trying to quit smoking.

step2_rag_chunks: Cravings for tobacco are temporary and usually last between 5 and 10 minutes. When a craving occurs, delay smoking and focus on another activity until the urge passes.

Patients who smoke their first cigarette within 30 minutes of waking up, or who smoke more than 15 cigarettes per day, often have strong physical nicotine dependence. For these patients, combining behavioral strategies with nicotine replacement therapy gives better results than behavioral strategies alone.
Many people smoke out of habit during specific routines such as after meals, while drinking tea or coffee, or when talking with friends.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the EXPLORATION phase. You know the basics. Now gently explore what situations or emotions make them smoke more — triggers like stress, meals, social settings, boredom. Do NOT suggest any strategies yet. Just understand their situation.

step2_rag_chunks: Many people smoke out of habit during specific routines such as after meals, while drinking tea or coffee, or when talking with friends.

Smoking is often tied to a habit loop â€” a cue triggers the urge, smoking is the routine, and relief is the reward. Understanding what your specific cues are is the first step. It could be a time of day, a place, an emotion, or even a person. Once we identify the cue, we can work on changing just the routine while keeping the relief.

Having even one person who knows you are trying to quit and supports you makes the process significantly easier. This is not about having someone watch over you â€” it is about having someone to call when the urge is strong, or someone who will not offer you a cigarette. Who in your life could be that person for you?

step2_rag_chunks: Keeping your hands busy with activities such as drawing, writing, or using a stress ball can help reduce the urge to smoke.

After meals, immediately introduce a competing behavior before the urge has a chance to form â€” get up from the table, brush your teeth, chew sugar-free gum, or take a short walk. The goal is to break the automatic link between finishing a meal and reaching for a cigarette by making a different behavior equally automatic over time.
Replacing smoking with another activity such as chewing gum, eating healthy snacks, or walking can help break the habit.

If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.
Support from friends, family, or healthcare professionals can improve the chances of successfully quitting smoking.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: The patient has signaled they are ready to act or is wrapping up. Your ONLY job is to give ONE warm closing sentence. Do NOT ask any question. Do NOT suggest anything new. Do NOT summarize what was discussed. Just acknowledge their readiness warmly and wish them well. Example: 'That sounds like a solid plan — take it one step at a time.' One sentence only. Nothing more.

step2_rag_chunks: If you have tried to quit before, that experience is actually useful information â€” not a failure. What worked even a little? What made it harder? Most people who successfully quit have tried several times before it sticks. Each attempt teaches you something about your own pattern.
Relapse can happen during the quitting process. Instead of feeling discouraged, treat it as a learning experience and continue trying to quit.

It is completely normal to want to quit and also feel unsure about it at the same time. Most people feel this way â€” one part of them wants to stop, another part is not ready. That conflict is not weakness, it is just how change works. It helps to talk about both sides openly rather than pushing the doubts aside.

step2_rag_chunks: Having even one person who knows you are trying to quit and supports you makes the process significantly easier. This is not about having someone watch over you â€” it is about having someone to call when the urge is strong, or someone who will not offer you a cigarette. Who in your life could be that person for you?
Planning ahead for difficult situations such as parties or social events can help prevent relapse.
Keeping a diary of triggers and cravings can help identify patterns and develop better coping strategies.
